metrics.narrative: ANU articulated qualitative pain around procurement inefficiency and desire to modernize P2P operations, but no quantified business case (ROI, cost savings, cycle-time reduction, headcount impact) has been built or shared. Per Next Step 18 Jun, deal team offered to 'assist with business case building,' confirming the commercial justification remains unquantified. Simon Bernhard stated on 12 May call that management resignations have delayed budget approval, implying the project lacks a defensible financial case to secure CFO/Chancellor sign-off during a governance crisis. The Canvas LMS breach (200 days downtime, 12 May call) elevated vendor security and reputation as evaluation criteria, but this is a risk-mitigation concern, not a quantified savings driver. Without a quantified value case, ANU cannot justify the $350k investment to the CFO, and the deal remains stalled at budget approval.

champion.narrative: Simon Bernhard (Strategic Procurement Manager, OpportunityContactRole) is the identified champion with developing strength. Evidence of advocacy: Simon drove the initial discovery and requirements scoping (09 Feb call and Next Step), stated ANU will 'definitely be going to tender' (12 May call), and committed to reviewing and sharing Zycus materials internally with Procurement, IT, and Security (09 Feb Next Step). He has maintained access to operational stakeholders (Harindu Jayakody, David Moran) but NOT to the Economic Buyer (CFO) or Executive Sponsor (Chancellor/Vice Chancellor). Champion strength is developing, not strong, because: (1) Simon lacks authority to unstall CFO/Chancellor budget approval—he is operational, not executive; (2) his engagement has cooled significantly since 12 May (58 days ago, last two-way touch), with rep follow-ups 11 Jun, 18 Jun, 30 Jun, 06 Jul, 09 Jul producing only a generic 'they will be in contact shortly' (06 Jul) with no substantive update; (3) no second champion or multi-threaded advocacy exists to de-risk if Simon's priority shifts during the governance crisis. Simon is at risk of being deprioritized or sidelined as ANU's leadership stabilizes and new COO/Chancellor reset priorities.

competition.narrative: Competing against Coupa (primary) and SAP Ariba (secondary) per Salesforce Competitors__c field and anticipated government RFP shortlist dynamics. No direct buyer quotes naming competitors have been logged on calls, but the formal Tenderlink RFP process (09 Feb Next Step) will attract both incumbents. Coupa and Ariba are standard government P2P shortlist vendors in Australia, and ANU's requirement for indigenous reseller preference (09 Feb Next Step) may favor vendors with established Australian partner ecosystems. Zycus differentiation: (1) Merlin agentic AI and Intake automation (Products: Merlin, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI, ANA) position against Coupa/Ariba's legacy workflow engines; (2) AU data residency at Macquarie DC and SOC compliance already delivered (09 Feb action completed); (3) ANU-led implementation approach addressing prior P2P project failure (vendor-led design risk). The wedge is Merlin's negotiation and intake intelligence as a Phase 2 AI-led differentiator, but this story has not been activated on any call yet. Risk: if the RFP scorecard is price-led or Coupa/Ariba undercut on cost, Zycus may lose on commercial grounds without a quantified ROI case to justify premium positioning.

identify_pain.narrative: ANU's pain centers on procurement inefficiency, lack of modern P2P automation, and the operational risk exposed by the Canvas LMS breach (200 days downtime, 12 May call). Simon Bernhard articulated on the 09 Feb call (inferred from Next Step) a 'desire to lead the project through changes in procurement and processing,' signaling operational frustration with current manual or legacy P2P workflows. The prior P2P project failure ('failed within three months because they had to adapt to the purchased technology') created change-management pain and fear of repeating vendor-led implementation mistakes. The Canvas LMS breach elevated security and reputation concerns, making vendor risk mitigation a new pain point. Pain ownership sits with Simon Bernhard (Strategic Procurement lead, operational pain) and David Moran (Director, Expenditure/Procurement, process pain), but the CFO (Economic Buyer, unnamed) must feel the financial or risk pain to justify the $350k investment—and that pain has not been quantified or articulated on any call.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are standard government procurement: RFP response, evaluation, shortlist, negotiation, MSA/SOW, legal review, and final CFO/Chancellor sign-off. ANU flagged indigenous reseller preference on 09 Feb, suggesting partner involvement may be required or advantageous. No Systems Integrator (SI) has been named or engaged. InfoSec review is embedded in the RFP evaluation (IT/Security stakeholders will review per 09 Feb Next Step), and SOC compliance documentation has been provided (09 Feb action completed). No current contract expiration date or forcing function is logged; this is a new P2P system procurement, not a renewal. The paper process is straightforward once the RFP is published, but the gate is budget approval—until CFO/Chancellor sign off, no RFP can be released and no contracting can begin. The governance crisis (Chancellor resignation 10 Jun, four council members out) has left the final approval authority vacant, creating indefinite delay risk.

decision_process.narrative: ANU is running a formal government tender process via Tenderlink (government tender portal) per 09 Feb Next Step. The process sequence is: (1) CFO and Chancellor budget approval to release RFP (currently stalled, 55+ days overdue from July target per 12 May Next Step); (2) publish RFP/RFQ on Tenderlink for vendor responses (buyer commitment, 09 Feb); (3) RFP evaluation by Procurement (Simon, Harindu) and IT/Security stakeholders (09 Feb Next Step: 'Simon to review + share internally with Procurement, IT, Security'); (4) shortlist, demos, negotiation; (5) final approval by CFO and Chancellor/Vice Chancellor (per 11 Jun Next Step). The approval chain is multi-level: operational (Simon, Harindu, David Moran) → CFO → Chancellor/Vice Chancellor. The process has stalled at step 1 (budget approval) due to governance crisis: Chancellor Julie Bishop resigned 10 Jun 2026, four council members stepped down, mandatory board elections triggered (11 Jun Next Step, 12 May call). No new Chancellor or Vice Chancellor has been named, leaving the final approval authority vacant. COO (new, name not stated) was expected to champion budget approval internally by July (12 May Next Step), but July has passed with no resolution.

decision_criteria.narrative: ANU's evaluation criteria are partially visible from the 09 Feb and 12 May calls and Next Step history. Key criteria include: (1) SOC compliance, data residency, AU hosting (Macquarie DC), and vendor security/reputation (elevated post-Canvas LMS breach, 12 May call); (2) modular licensing and phased implementation approach (big-bang vs module-by-module) with transparent timelines (09 Feb Next Step); (3) workflow controls, stopgates, thresholds, PO-invoice match, and reporting capabilities (09 Feb Next Step RFP template requirements); (4) indigenous reseller preference and partner/reseller model (09 Feb Next Step); (5) ANU-led design and change management to avoid prior P2P project failure ('failed within three months because they had to adapt to the purchased technology,' 09 Feb call). The formal RFP/RFQ on Tenderlink will carry the full criteria, but these confirmed elements align with typical government procurement scorecards: security, functionality, implementation risk, and value.
